mnemonics.title: BFS-DFS Dance in Hopcroft-Karp
mnemonics.content: BFS Builds Layers, DFS Dances through paths. BFS finds distance to free vertices, DFS finds augmenting paths from each free vertex in the left set. B = Building layers, D = Direct paths.

mnemonics.title: HALL: Have A Lovely Little Matching
mnemonics.content: Hall's Marriage Theorem: For every subset S of left vertices, |N(S)| ≥ |S|. Remember: Have A Lovely Little set — the neighborhood (N) must be at least as large as your set (S).

mnemonics.title: Augmenting Path = Flip the Strip
mnemonics.content: An augmenting path alternates between unmatched and matched edges. Remember: Imagine a path as a strip with alternating colors (unmatched/matched). To augment, flip the colors — unmatched becomes matched and vice versa.

mnemonics.title: KÖNIG's Krown
mnemonics.content: König's Theorem: In bipartite graphs, size of maximum matching = size of minimum vertex cover. Remember: The KÖNIG gets a KROWN — think of the vertex cover as 'crowning' the minimum vertices that cover all edges.

mnemonics.title: Perfect = Complete Coverage
mnemonics.content: A perfect matching covers ALL vertices in both sets. Remember: PERFECT = Pairs Every node from Right side To every node on Left side Completely.

mnemonics.title: Greedy Fails, Paths Save
mnemonics.content: A greedy approach (matching as you see free vertices) doesn't guarantee maximum matching. Remember: Greedy Finds Errors — you need augmenting paths to fix mistakes and reach optimality.

acronyms.usage: Algorithm name for finding maximum matching in O(E√V) time. Use when dealing with unweighted bipartite graphs and need efficient solution.
Used in Hopcroft-Karp to build layered graph and find distance to free vertices in right set. First phase of each iteration.
Used in Hopcroft-Karp to find augmenting paths from free vertices in left set. Second phase of each iteration.
Set of all vertices adjacent to at least one vertex in S. Used in Hall's Theorem: |N(S)| ≥ |S| for all subsets S of left vertices.
A set of edges with no common vertices. Always denote current matching as M when discussing algorithms.
Path that starts and ends at free vertices, alternates between unmatched and matched edges. Used to increase matching size by 1.
Set of vertices that touches every edge. In bipartite graphs, |Maximum Matching| = |Minimum Vertex Cover| (König's Theorem).

visualTips.visualization: Draw two vertical lines/columns — left set (L) on the left, right set (R) on the right. All edges only go from L to R, never within the same set. Like two teams sitting on opposite sides of a table.
Imagine drawing bold lines or circles around paired vertices. A matching shows as disjoint connections — no two edges share a vertex. Think of it as creating pairs for a dance.

Use two colors — green for unmatched edges, red for matched edges. An augmenting path shows alternating green-red-green pattern starting and ending with green (unmatched). The path forms a 'snake' from left free vertex to right free vertex.

Think of levels/levels like a tree. BFS builds layers: Level 0 = free left vertices, Level 1 = their neighbors, Level 2 = neighbors of those, etc. Only explore edges that could lead to shortest augmenting paths.

After finding maximum matching, find minimum vertex cover using König's algorithm: Start from all free left vertices, explore alternating paths, mark all reachable vertices. Left unmarked + Right marked = Minimum Vertex Cover.
In BFS phase of HK, build a tree starting from free left vertices. Edges alternate: unmatched from left, matched from right. This tree helps find all shortest augmenting paths efficiently.

analogies.analogy: Job Assignment Problem: Imagine N job seekers on the left and M jobs on the right. An edge exists if a person is qualified for a job. Maximum matching finds the maximum number of people who can get jobs — no person gets two jobs, no job goes to two people.
Speed Dating Event: When both sets have equal size, a perfect matching means everyone finds a partner. Every person on the left is matched with exactly one on the right.

Roommate Swap: You have some roommates paired, but one person is unpaired (A). They know someone (B) who's paired but unhappy. B's current partner (C) knows another person (D) who is free. The chain A→B→C→D lets everyone move and frees A — this is like flipping the matched/unmatched status along the path.

Group Dates: For any group of k women (left set) to all find husbands, there must be at least k men (right set) they collectively know. If a group of 5 women only knows 3 men total, someone won't get a match — condition fails.

Security Guards: Think of vertices as security guards and edges as rooms. A vertex cover is the minimum number of guards needed to watch all rooms (edges). The maximum number of non-overlapping pairs (matching) equals the minimum guards needed.

Group Interviews: Instead of interviewing candidates one-by-one (O(V·E)), do batch interviews in rounds. First round: BFS maps out all shortest paths. Second round: DFS actually conducts multiple interviews simultaneously. Each round increases matching by maximum possible amount.
